title: Lesson 4: Overview of the Bible
description: A guided tour of the book in your hands. The Bible is not a single book. It is a library of 66 books written across thousands of years, and learning how it is organized will change the way you read it.

blocks.text: In the last lesson you explored what it means to truly belong to a church family. You learned that the Christian life is not meant to be lived alone. But here is what holds that family together: the Word of God. In this lesson and the two that follow, you are going to get to know that Word in a way you never have before. Think of it as three lessons that build on each other. This lesson gives you the map. The next one takes you on the road trip. And the one after that gives you the skill to dig deeper into any passage on your own.
A Library, Not a Single Book

blocks.alt: Library filled with books on curved wooden shelves

blocks.text: Many people think of the Bible as one big book. But the Bible is actually a library. It contains 66 individual books written by roughly 40 different authors over a span of about 1,500 years. There are kings, shepherds, fishermen, doctors, tax collectors, and prophets among the writers. They wrote in different countries, in different centuries, in different circumstances. And yet the whole library tells one unified story from beginning to end.
That is remarkable. No human editor planned this across fifteen centuries. The unity of Scripture is one of the strongest evidences that God Himself was behind every page. As Paul wrote to Timothy:
All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work.

Breathed out by God. That means when you open the Bible, you are not reading someone's personal journal or philosophical opinion. You are reading words that the living God intended for you to have. And because He organized this library with purpose, understanding how it is structured will help you read it with much greater confidence.
4.1 - Before this lesson, how would you describe your familiarity with the Bible? Do you feel confident finding your way around it, or does it sometimes feel overwhelming?
The Two Testaments

The Bible is divided into two major parts: the Old Testament and the New Testament. The word "testament" means covenant, a binding promise between God and His people. The Old Testament tells the story of God creating the world, choosing a people (Israel), giving them His law, and promising that a Savior would come. The New Testament tells the story of that Savior arriving in Jesus, the birth of the church, and the future hope of all things being made new.

The Old Testament has 39 books. The New Testament has 27. Together they form the complete Word of God. Some people struggle with the Old Testament because it feels distant or confusing. But here is the key: the Old Testament is not a different story with a different God. It is the first part of the same story, and every page of it points forward to Jesus. You cannot fully understand the New Testament without the Old, and you cannot fully understand the Old without the New.
Below is every book of the Bible arranged on a shelf by section. Tap any book to learn what its section is about.
4.2 - Which section of the Old Testament are you most drawn to or curious about? Which one feels the most unfamiliar?
4.3 - Which section of the New Testament has been most meaningful to you so far in your faith journey? Is there a section you have never explored?
When Josiah Found the Book
There is a story in the Old Testament that shows just how powerful it is to encounter the Word of God, even when you have never heard it before.

King Josiah came to the throne when he was only eight years old. He grew up in a nation that had completely abandoned God. The temple was in ruins. Idols filled the land. The people had no idea what God's Word even said, because the scrolls of the Law had been lost for generations. Then one day, while the temple was being repaired, the high priest found the Book of the Law buried in the rubble.
When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes.

Josiah was undone. He had never heard these words before, and when he did, everything changed. He wept. He repented. He led the entire nation back to God. One encounter with the Word of God was enough to redirect the course of a kingdom.

That is what the Bible does. It is not just information about God. It is an encounter with God. And now that you know how this library is organized, you are better prepared to find your way through it, one section at a time, one book at a time, one passage at a time.
4.4 - Has there been a time when a passage of Scripture hit you the way it hit Josiah, where the words stopped you in your tracks and changed something inside you? What happened?
A Word on Translations

The Bible was originally written in Hebrew (most of the Old Testament), Aramaic (small portions), and Greek (the New Testament). Because most of us do not read those languages, we rely on English translations. There are many good ones, and choosing a translation can feel confusing. Here is a simple way to think about it.

Some translations prioritize word-for-word accuracy, staying as close to the original language as possible. The ESV (English Standard Version) and NASB are examples of this. Others prioritize thought-for-thought clarity, translating the meaning of each sentence in a way that feels more natural to a modern reader. The NIV and NLT are examples of this. Both approaches are faithful to Scripture. A word-for-word translation is excellent for study. A thought-for-thought translation is excellent for reading and understanding quickly.

This course uses the ESV, which gives you a reliable, readable translation that stays very close to the original words. Whatever translation you use, the most important thing is that you are reading the Bible consistently. The best translation is the one you will actually open.
4.5 - What Bible translation do you currently read? Have you ever compared the same passage in two different translations? If so, what did you notice?
Lesson 4 Review

You now have the map. The Bible is a library of 66 books arranged in clear sections: Law, History, Poetry, Prophets, Gospels, Acts, Letters, and Revelation. The Old Testament tells the story of God creating, choosing, and promising. The New Testament tells the story of God fulfilling every promise in Jesus. In the next lesson you will take the road trip through the one big story that ties all of these books together.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
